column1:
SYSTEM:
You are a clinical documentation specialist for internal medicine in Nova Scotia, Canada. Current date is: {CURRENT_DATE}. You will receive a long mixed-quality bundle (transcripts, referral letters, prior notes, labs, imaging, ECGs, portal text). First, read the entire bundle, then internally clean and normalize it before writing: remove non-clinical filler and portal/UI noise (e.g., "Click here...", "other clinicians who viewed...", headers/footers, routing metadata, duplicated blocks). De-duplicate repeated content and keep the most complete copy. Identity and consistency: preserve patient name/DOB/sex/PHN exactly as provided and keep them consistent; never guess missing identifiers; do not change age unless DOB is provided and age is logically derivable. Temporal provenance: extract all explicit dates/times; organize facts chronologically; clearly separate current-encounter information from prior records; never present prior data as current; never create future dates beyond {CURRENT_DATE}; when clinically relevant, include the date or relative timing (e.g., prior, earlier this year) for key facts in History of Present Illness, Investigations, and Impression; do not collapse information from different dates into a single undated statement; if the current encounter date is not explicit, use {CURRENT_DATE} only as an ordering anchor and do not claim events occurred today unless explicitly stated. Conflict handling: if sources disagree, do not silently resolve; state the conflict and attribute each to its dated source. Physical exam (encounter-bound): include exam findings only if explicitly documented as performed in the current encounter; if exam is explicitly deferred, write "Physical exam: deferred" and do not include any exam findings from prior encounters even if present; if telephone/virtual with no exam, write "Physical exam: not performed"; if absent with no indication, write "Physical exam: not documented". Vitals: include vitals only if explicitly documented for the current encounter; do not infer, estimate, or carry forward prior vitals. Allergies: list exactly as documented; if not present, write "Allergies: not documented"; never assume NKDA. Medications: the most recent dated medication list in the input is the authoritative current list; do not invent drugs, doses, routes, or frequencies; use generic names only; format each medication as "Drug dose unit route frequency" (spell out route and frequency), one per line; medication name integrity: if another part of the bundle mentions a medication that does not clearly match the authoritative list and cannot be safely normalized, do not guess—list it under "Medication uncertainties:" at the end with "[NEED MORE INFO: exact drug name/dose]"; use Medication uncertainties only when necessary and re-check the source if more than 3 items appear there. Devices/implants/proper nouns: for prosthetic valves, pacemakers, stents, implants, manufacturers, model numbers, and sizes, preserve exact wording from the source; do not paraphrase or substitute similar-sounding names; if unclear, flag "[VERIFY: device/implant specification]" rather than guessing. Investigations: in the Investigations section, list only relevant tests with dates and numeric results/units exactly as provided; do not include reference ranges; do not label results as high/low/abnormal; do not interpret in that section (interpretation and severity emphasis belong in Impression). Numeric plausibility safeguard: do not correct values, but if a value-unit pair is physiologically implausible or magnitude seems inconsistent with typical units, flag "[VERIFY: implausible value/unit for X - check source]". False reassurance guard: do not write "ruled out", "no evidence of", or definitive exclusions unless the source uses equivalent definitive language; when a negative test does not definitively exclude an important diagnosis and that limitation is clinically relevant, acknowledge the limitation in Impression using cautious language. Impression requirements: begin with the primary problem or most clinically important diagnosis driving management, followed by secondary active problems in descending clinical importance; avoid long equal-weight lists; emphasize critical red flags and high-risk findings in Impression using appropriate clinical language derived from provided data. Plan authority and safety addendum: if the current encounter includes an explicit clinician plan or next step (including narrative statements), it must be reflected as the primary Plan and must not be overridden by older plans or generic best practice; before finalizing Plan, check for internal conflicts (mutually incompatible recommendations) and if found, flag "[CONFLICT IN PLAN: ...]" rather than listing both; Addendum (safety) may be used only for clearly justified safety gaps based on current-encounter data and limited to life-threatening omissions, critical allergy or interaction conflicts, or mandatory safety monitoring for explicitly documented high-risk therapies; do not add generic best practices or alternative pathways. Severe infection specificity: if antibiotics are documented, name them explicitly; if severe infection or sepsis is clearly present but antibiotics are not documented, do not invent—flag "[NEED MORE INFO: antibiotics selected]". Urgency wording: specify timing (emergent, urgent, routine) only if explicitly stated in the source or if a time-critical syndrome is clearly supported by current-encounter data; otherwise do not invent timing and use "[NEED MORE INFO: timing]" if required. Responsibility attribution: do not assign responsibility implicitly (e.g., "we will monitor", "team will follow") unless explicitly stated in the source; when responsibility is unclear, use neutral phrasing. Output formatting: plain text only (no markdown, no tables, no separators). Section titles are simple words ending with a colon on their own line. Bullets are allowed. Use numerals not words. Vitals format if present: BP 132/60 mmHg, HR 75 bpm, RR 16/min, Temp 36.8°C, SpO2 98% RA. FINAL OUTPUT CONTROL: you may think freely, but the final note saved by the app must appear only between the markers "FINAL NOTE START" and "FINAL NOTE END"; do not include anything outside those markers in the final note.

USER:
Write a consult note answering the referral question for the current encounter. Focus on the active problem(s) relevant to this referral and today’s safety or management; exclude chronic conditions unless they directly affect today’s assessment, risk stratification, medication choice, or disposition. Do not copy forward long chronic problem lists from old notes. Use this section order: Patient ID, History of Present Illness, Past Medical History, Medications, Allergies, Family and Social History, Physical exam, Investigations, Impression, Plan. In Impression, start with the primary problem driving management and then list secondary active issues in descending importance with brief rationale. In Plan, reflect the clinician’s stated plan if present; include specific actionable steps supported by current data, and assign responsibility only when explicitly stated in the source.

FINAL NOTE START  
Patient ID: Karim Shaloh, 42 years  

Subjective:  
The patient reports new onset severe chest pain prompting presentation to the emergency department. He denies any prior medical conditions or similar episodes previously. DR: MBAH, CHIKAODINAKA S MD FAMILY PHYSICIAN: GILLIS,JOSEPH M MD COPIES TO: GILLIS,JOSEPH M MD; MBAH,CHIKAODINAKA S MD CATEGORIE(S): MAGNETIC RESONANCE IMAGING PROCEDURE(S): LSPINE WWO WITH 6PULVC MRI of lumbar spine (enhanced) Indication: Fever for 5 days. Blood in urine culture was positive for Staphylococcus aureus. WBC 29, CRP 298. Acute back pain. Discitis, osteomyelitis or abscess? Findings: Comparison is made to multiple prior studies, including CT 11/11/2021. Extremely limited examination due to significant motion artifact in this patient who has constant twitching and shaking in his legs. Allowing for this limitation, AP alignment of the lumbar spine appears within normal limits. Vertebral body heights and disc spaces are maintained. Conus appears to terminate normally posterior to L1. No definite signal abnormality in the visualized cord. No definite marrow signal abnormality nor abnormal signal in the visualized disc spaces to suggest discitis or osteomyelitis. No discrete fluid collection or abscess is identified. Postcontrast sequences are limited by significant motion artifact. No definite abnormal enhancement is identified. There is multilevel degenerative disc disease. There are mild annular bulges at L12, L23 and L5S1 that do not appear to cause significant stenosis. Moderate bilateral hypertrophic facet joint osteoarthritis at L45 and L5S1 with associated bilateral facet joint effusions. No convincing spinal canal stenosis or foraminal narrowing. Vaguely wedgeshaped regions of hypoenhancement partly imaged in the lower pole of the right kidney and also the upper pole of the left kidney. Impression: Limited examination with significant motion artifact does not demonstrate any convincing evidence of spondylodiscitis, osteomyelitis or paravertebral or other abscess. Mild to moderate multilevel degenerative changes with facet joint osteoarthritis and effusions in the lower lumbar spine. Partly imaged kidneys demonstrate bilateral somewhat wedgeshaped regions of decreased perfusion. In this clinical context, the appearance raises the possibility of pyelonephritis. FINAL NOTE START  
Patient ID: Arthur Donald Belliveau, DOB 29/10/1963, PHN 0008882730  

Chief Complaint: Fever, altered mental status, hypotension requiring ICU transfer.  

History of Present Illness:  
On 26 Oct 2025 the patient noted rising temperature (up to 38 °C) and became increasingly disoriented. Vital sign trends were not recorded in this encounter, but the attending physician reports “BP is becoming soft” and the patient is “very confused.” A blood culture drawn 26 Oct 2025 yielded *Staphylococcus aureus* (reported 20:35 hrs). Subsequent cultures collected later that evening remained negative. The patient has been admitted to the hospital for several days prior to this episode. Over the past week laboratory data demonstrate persistent leukocytosis (WBC 11–12 × 10⁹/L), markedly elevated C‑reactive protein (>200 mg/L), and a modest rise in serum creatinine (≈80 µmol/L). Electrolyte abnormalities include hyponatremia (Na = 134–142 mmol/L) and hypokalemia (K = 3.2–3.6 mmol/L). No imaging or physical exam was performed during this admission; all relevant investigations are listed below.  

Relevant Past Medical History:  
• Heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (LVEF 40%) secondary to dilated cardiomyopathy and severe aortic regurgitation; bioprosthetic Edwards Lifesciences 27 mm CE Perimount Magna Ease aortic valve implanted Aug 24 2020.  
• Hypertension, dyslipidemia, former smoker (60 pack‑years, quit 11 yrs ago); current marijuana vaping 3 g/day.  
• Alcohol use disorder – abstinent ≥12 yrs.  
• Asthma (history of bronchial asthma).  
• Chronic right chest, neck, and spinal pain post‑trauma (14 yrs ago).  

Medications: Not documented in the most recent dated medication list.  

Allergies: No known allergies.  

Family and Social History: Lives alone with his dog; receives disability benefits under workers’ compensation for traumatic injury.  

Physical Examination: Not performed during this admission; therefore “Physical exam: not performed”.  

Investigations:  
Laboratory (most recent dates):
